title: Sorting and Binary Search
learningObjectives: Explain Sorting and Binary Search with interview-grade clarity
Implement Sorting and Binary Search solutions in clean Java
Choose the right pattern and justify complexity
Handle boundary cases without brittle logic
Use this topic to solve coding-round problems confidently

sections.title: Why Sorting and Binary Search matters
sections.content:
Sorting and binary search are foundational optimization tools; using them correctly often changes interview outcomes.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Choosing sort strategy and writing robust binary search variants.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 26, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Sorting and Binary Search
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Sorting and binary search are foundational optimization tools; using them correctly often changes interview outcomes.

### Core interview mental model
For Sorting and Binary Search, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Sorting and Binary Search are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Binary search** requires monotonic predicate — not only on arrays but on answer space (“min capacity to ship packages”).

**Invariant style:** define `[lo, hi)` or `[lo, hi]` and never mix — off-by-one bugs dominate.

**Sorting stability** matters when secondary keys exist — `Collections.sort` guarantees stable merge sort / TimSort behaviour.

**Custom comparators** in `Arrays.sort(T[], Comparator)` — avoid violating transitivity.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Mid calculation overflow in binary search on large bounds.
Using binary search on unsorted input.
Infinite loops from incorrect low/high update rules.
Not distinguishing lower_bound vs upper_bound style goals.
Overusing O(n log n) sort when linear approach exists.

sections.title: Exercise - Sorting and Binary Search Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Sorting and Binary Search**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: Stable sort definition?
conceptual.answer: Equal elements keep relative order—important for multi-key sorts.

conceptual.question: Which sorts are stable in Java?
conceptual.answer: TimSort (objects), merge sort stable; typical quicksort unstable.

conceptual.question: Quicksort average vs worst?
conceptual.answer: Average O(n log n); worst O(n²) bad pivot—randomize or median-of-three.

conceptual.question: Merge sort properties?
conceptual.answer: O(n log n) worst, O(n) extra space, stable.

conceptual.question: Insertion sort when good?
conceptual.answer: Small n nearly sorted—O(n) best case adaptive.

conceptual.question: Counting sort radix sort?
conceptual.answer: Linear when range constraints—not comparison lower bound.

conceptual.question: Binary search precondition?
conceptual.answer: Monotonic ordering on indexed access—array sorted or rotated variants with care.

conceptual.question: Find rotation point in rotated sorted array?
Lower_bound / upper_bound concepts?
conceptual.answer: First position where predicate true—Arrays.binarySearch variants.

conceptual.question: External sort?
conceptual.answer: Merge sorted chunks from disk—streaming large data.

conceptual.question: Parallel sort Arrays.parallelSort?
conceptual.answer: ForkJoin for large primitive arrays—speed vs overhead.

conceptual.question: Inversion count?
conceptual.answer: Merge sort augmentation—count cross inversions while merging.

conceptual.question: Selection vs quickselect?
conceptual.answer: Quickselect finds kth element average O(n).

conceptual.question: Compare sort vs heap for “top K”.
conceptual.answer: Heap O(n log k) often beats full O(n log n) sort when k small.

codeBased.question: Binary search first >= target (lower bound).
codeBased.answer: lo/hi loop variants—know one by heart.

codeBased.question: Merge two sorted arrays in place (with extra space at end).
Count inversions merge step.
codeBased.answer: When a[i]>b[j] add remaining left elements to count.

codeBased.question: Search insert position in sorted array.
Stable counting sort for small range integers.
Parallel sort threshold?
codeBased.answer: JDK uses size threshold before parallel—tunable implementation detail.

seniorScenario.question: DB ORDER BY vs app sort—when which?
seniorScenario.answer: Large data: sort in DB with index; paginate—avoid loading all rows.

seniorScenario.question: Sort huge file on disk—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: External merge sort chunks; k-way merge.

seniorScenario.question: Non-transitive Comparator bug—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: IllegalArgumentException from TimSort or inconsistent ordering—deadly in production.

seniorScenario.question: Locale-sensitive sort mismatch?
seniorScenario.answer: Use Collator; binary sort wrong for human display.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed sort (MapReduce)?
seniorScenario.answer: Shuffle by key, sort partitions—different paradigm.

seniorScenario.question: Time-series data out-of-order ingest?
seniorScenario.answer: Sort windows or use data structures accepting disorder.

wrongAnswers: Binary search works on unsorted array if you check each step.
Quicksort worst case cannot be avoided.
Merge sort is in-place with O(1) extra space typically.
Stable sort means equal elements swap randomly.
Binary search always returns an index when element missing.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Day 26 Focus | Quick Reminder | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Sorting and Binary Search | Choose pattern before coding | Random brute-force wandering |
| Correctness | Invariant + edge checks | Dry-run once | Off-by-one / null-state miss |
| Complexity | Derive, do not guess | Mention time + space | Incomplete complexity answers |
| Communication | Explain decisions | Use trade-off statement | Generic textbook responses |
| Quality | Readable Java methods | Stable naming + small units | Clever but fragile logic |
